That “Doggy Breath” You’ve Gotten Used To Isn’t Normal. It’s a Warning.

It’s the first thing most owners notice, and the first thing most vets wish they’d caught sooner. Nearly 80% of dogs show signs of dental disease by age three.1 Not the rare, unlucky ones. Most of them.

And it almost always starts the same quiet way: a little bad breath, a faint yellow film along the gumline, nothing that looks like an emergency. But that smell is bacteria. It builds into plaque, plaque hardens into tartar, and once it’s under the gumline it does not clean itself off. Left alone, removing it stops being a wipe and starts being a procedure at the vet, under anesthesia that gets riskier as your dog ages.

Veterinary research has linked advanced dental disease in dogs to added strain on the heart, liver and kidneys over time.2 The mouth is just where you see it first. Caught early, though, this is one of the most preventable problems your dog will ever have.

See the 30-Second Fix →

You Bought the Toothbrush. You Lasted About a Week.

Nobody skips their dog’s teeth because they don’t care. They skip it because the routine they were handed is a wrestling match. You corner him, he clamps his jaw, you get one panicked pass across the front teeth, and you both quietly decide never again.

“

Most owners quit brushing in the first two weeks. Not because it doesn’t work. Because it’s a fight nobody wins twice a day.

NL
Dr. Nicole LeibmanVeterinarian · Roofus’s vet

So the fix was never a better toothbrush. It was skipping the brush entirely.

Why Nothing You’ve Tried Has Actually Worked

✗

Brushing

A fight your dog usually wins. It only reaches the gumline if he holds still, and most won’t. The routine dies in a week.

✗

Dental chews

A bribe, not a clean. Dogs gnaw the easy parts and leave the back teeth and gumline completely untouched.

✗

Water additives

Float right past the problem. Nothing physically lifts the film off the tooth, so the buildup keeps hardening.

The one thing they all miss

Every fix above skips the same step: actually scrubbing the plaque off the tooth. That is the whole job, and it is the one thing Roofus is built to do.

Roofus is built around Buffbead, a dual-sided fabric the brand engineered in-house over 18 months.4 It does the two things a toothbrush does, without the toothbrush.

The textured side

Scrubs

The raised Buffbead texture lifts plaque and tartar off the tooth and along the gumline, the way bristles would.

Polishes

The other side wipes the surface clean and leaves the breath fresh, finishing the whole thing in one pass.

Meet Roofus’s Veterinarian

Dr. Nicole Leibman DVM · Roofus’s veterinarian
Roofus’s veterinarian

Dr. Leibman spent years watching the same thing happen in her exam room: owners who genuinely cared, holding a toothbrush they’d given up on. The problem was never the owner. It was a routine built to fail.

So she got behind a different approach, built around the one thing that survives real life: a thirty-second habit a dog won’t fight. Plant-based, lick-safe, and dual-sided to actually reach the gumline.

“Consistency is what protects the mouth, not the fanciest ingredient list. If it takes thirty seconds and the dog doesn’t fight it, owners do it every day.”

The Difference Daily Care Makes

Plaque and tartar build up slowly, and they come off the same way: a little every day. Thirty seconds is the whole habit.

Before · visible buildup
After · daily wiping

Questions People Have First

Is it safe if he licks it?
Yes. The formula is plant-based, alcohol-free and lick-safe, which matters because most dogs will lick their lips the second you’re done. It’s designed for exactly that.
Does it really work without brushing?
That’s the whole idea. The Buffbead texture does the scrubbing a brush would do, on your finger, so you reach the teeth and gumline without the fight that makes people quit brushing.
How is it different from dental chews?
A chew is chewed, not aimed. Dogs work the easy spots and leave the back teeth and gumline untouched. A wipe lets you physically clean the places buildup actually starts.
How soon will I notice fresher breath?
Many owners notice a difference within the first couple of weeks of daily use. Consistency is what does it, thirty seconds a day beats a big effort once in a while.
How many wipes come in a canister, and how long does one last?
Each canister has 60 dual-sided wipes, about two months of once-daily use for one dog. On the Buy 2 Get 2 bundle you get four canisters, roughly eight months.
Which flavor should I choose?
Peanut Butter is the most popular and the easiest to win over a reluctant dog. Minty Fresh leans into fresh breath, and Unscented is best for picky or sensitive dogs. On this page you can mix flavors across your bundle.
Is it safe for puppies and senior dogs?
Yes. The wipe is gentle and there’s no restraint or hard brushing involved, which makes it easier on puppies still learning and on older dogs with sensitive gums. Start slow and let them get used to your finger first.
How often should I use it?
Once a day is the sweet spot, ideally around the same time so it becomes a habit. Plaque re-forms daily, so daily removal is what keeps it from hardening into tartar.
Does this replace professional vet cleanings?
No, and we won’t pretend it does. Roofus is daily prevention that helps keep buildup from starting. Dogs with existing tartar under the gumline may still need a cleaning. Think of this as the habit that makes those visits less frequent.
What if it doesn’t work for my dog?
You’re covered by a 60-day breath-back guarantee. Use it daily for two months, and if his breath isn’t better, contact the team for a full refund.

Thirty Seconds a Day Is the Whole Routine.

Works Cited

  1. Prevalence of periodontal disease in dogs by age three: American Veterinary Dental College and American Veterinary Medical Association pet dental health materials.
  2. Association between advanced periodontal disease and systemic strain on the heart, liver and kidneys in dogs: veterinary dentistry literature. Framed as a documented association, not a claim that dental wipes treat or prevent systemic disease.
  3. Professional canine dental cleaning cost range and age-related anesthesia considerations: general veterinary cost references and AAHA anesthesia guidance.
  4. Buffbead dual-sided fabric and 18-month in-house development: Roofus product information, roofuspet.com.

Roofus Dental Finger Wipes support daily dental hygiene and fresh breath. They are not a treatment for periodontal disease and do not replace a professional cleaning or veterinary care your dog may need. Statements about dental disease prevalence and systemic health reflect general veterinary research, not claims about this product.
